questions.scenario: During a release storm, the Jenkins UI freezes while two Docker builds and a Maven test run on the built-in node. Three agents are online, but several pipelines use agent any. What is the architectural fix?

options.text: Add more built-in-node executors so the controller clears the queue faster.
Set controller executors to zero and route stages to capability-labeled agents.
Increase controller heap until Docker and Maven no longer compete.
Keep one controller executor for emergency builds and restrict Docker or Maven work through job naming conventions.

questions.explanation: The controller owns coordination and durable Jenkins state; build commands belong on agents. Zero controller executors plus explicit agent labels removes CPU, disk, and arbitrary build activity from the controller. More executors or heap preserve the unsafe boundary. See [Jenkins controller isolation](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/security/controller-isolation/).

questions.scenario: The controller VM is lost, but the team has a consistent backup of JENKINS_HOME and can recreate its agent machines. Which recovery plan reflects where Jenkins keeps durable state?

options.text: Restore agent workspaces first because they contain the authoritative job definitions.
Restore job definitions from source control, then reconstruct build history from archived agent workspaces.
Restore only the controller configuration files, then let agents repopulate credentials metadata and build records.
Restore the controller state from JENKINS_HOME, then reconnect or recreate agents for execution capacity.

questions.explanation: JENKINS_HOME contains controller configuration, jobs, plugins, credentials metadata, and build records. Agents provide workspaces and executors but do not become the source of truth for Jenkins coordination. See [Jenkins Docker installation and data paths](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/installing/docker/).

questions.scenario: Build VMs sit behind NAT. They can open outbound HTTPS to jenkins.summit.example, but the controller cannot initiate SSH into their subnet. Security will not approve a bastion or any new inbound path, and the reverse proxy already supports WebSocket upgrades. Which connection mode fits these constraints?

options.text: Controller-launched SSH agents over port 22.
Static agents that poll the build queue through browser sessions.
Inbound agents that connect outward through WebSocket over the Jenkins HTTPS endpoint.
An SSH bastion that allows the controller to launch agents through the private subnet.

questions.explanation: The reachable direction is from agent to controller, so an inbound agent fits. WebSocket uses the existing HTTPS path and avoids a separate inbound agent TCP port. SSH requires the opposite network path, and controller execution is unrelated. See [Jenkins exposed services and ports](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/security/services/).

questions.scenario: A pipeline compiles Java, builds a container image, and creates a Windows installer. The agent fleet exposes linux-maven, linux-docker, and windows labels. Which Jenkinsfile shape keeps privileges and tools separated?

options.text: Use agent none at pipeline level and select the required capability label on each stage.
Use one top-level `linux-docker` agent and send the Windows packaging command to a remote Windows host.
Use a top-level Linux agent, then override only the installer stage with the `windows` label.
Name individual machines in each Jenkinsfile instead of using labels.

questions.explanation: Stage-level capability labels let each task reach only the tools and privileges it needs. A top-level Linux allocation is insufficient because Maven and Docker are distinct capabilities in this fleet, and invoking Windows remotely bypasses Jenkins placement. Overriding only the Windows stage still leaves the two Linux stages coupled to one unspecified host. See [Jenkins Pipeline agent syntax](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/pipeline/syntax/#agent).

questions.scenario: Eight builds wait with the queue reason that no linux && docker executor is available. Maven and Windows agents are idle. The Docker agents are fully utilized. What should capacity planning focus on?

options.text: Add the `docker` label to idle Linux agents and install Docker only when a queued build first lands on them.
Remove the `docker` requirement from the waiting stages but continue running image-build commands on the general Linux pool.
Increase executors on the current Docker hosts without first checking CPU, memory, and disk contention.
Increase genuine Docker-capable capacity or reduce demand for that label after measuring host limits.

questions.explanation: The queue shows Docker-capability starvation, not a lack of total agents. Adding a label without preparing the host misrepresents capability, and removing the requirement sends image commands to unsuitable machines. Increasing executors may help only after host limits show the current machines can safely carry more concurrent builds. See [Jenkins managing nodes](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/managing/nodes/).

questions.scenario: The controller UI pauses for several seconds every afternoon. Heap is 85 percent used, GC logs show frequent long full collections, builds already stay off the controller, retained-build counts are within policy, and disk latency is normal. The host still has memory headroom. What is the next responsible tuning step?

options.text: Reduce build-history retention and wait for the next afternoon pause before making any heap change.
Increase heap gradually from evidence, then review plugins and retained builds.
Restart the controller on a schedule before the afternoon peak while leaving heap and plugin load unchanged.
Double heap immediately and leave little host memory for the operating system page cache.

questions.explanation: The evidence points to controller heap pressure after execution, retention, and disk causes were checked, and the host has headroom. A measured increase is the next controlled change, followed by continued plugin and retention review. Waiting through another known pause extends impact, while an immediate doubling can starve native memory and the page cache. See [Jenkins management and system information](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/managing/).

questions.scenario: The platform team is reviewing controller reliability after queue delays, agent disconnects, and a controller memory incident. Which controls belong in the production Jenkins architecture review?

options.text: Keep controller executors at zero and place build tools on agents.
Use persistent workspaces across trusted and untrusted jobs, but clear source files after each build.
Choose agent connection direction from the real network path and proxy behavior.
Use a broad `build` label for all agents and let each pipeline install missing capabilities at runtime.
Monitor queue reasons, agent health, heap and GC behavior, and controller disk separately.

questions.explanation: A healthy design separates controller and execution, matches connection mode to topology, and uses evidence from the correct boundary. Shared untrusted workspaces leak state, while hardware names make pipelines brittle and hide capabilities. See [Jenkins managing nodes](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/managing/nodes/) and [controller isolation](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/security/controller-isolation/).
